name: Global Economics & Politics
description: An interdisciplinary exploration of how economics and politics intersect on the world stage — international trade, development, geopolitics, international organizations, and global challenges.

modules.title: Introduction: Economics Meets Politics
modules.titleZh: 导论：经济与政治的交汇
modules.objectives: Define political economy; compare market, command, and mixed economic systems; explain why states and markets often conflict.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Economics #1 — Introduction to Economics
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — The difference between capitalism and socialism
modules.assignment: Select two countries — one with a market economy and one with a command economy. Compare their GDP per capita, human development index, and political freedoms in a 300-word analysis.

modules.title: International Trade Theory
modules.objectives: Explain absolute and comparative advantage; analyze the roles of tariffs, subsidies, and the WTO in shaping trade flows.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Economics #33 — Comparative Advantage & Trade
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Comparative Advantage & Gains from Trade
modules.assignment: Using comparative advantage theory, explain why the United States imports textiles from Bangladesh even though the US can produce them. Include a simple two-country, two-good table.

modules.title: Globalization
modules.objectives: Identify the causes and phases of globalization; evaluate the benefits and costs for developed and developing nations; analyze the role of multinational corporations.
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How global supply chains work
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Gains from Trade reading & questions
modules.assignment: Trace one product (e.g., a smartphone or a T-shirt) through its global supply chain. Identify at least four countries involved and explain the role of each. Evaluate one social or environmental cost of this supply chain.

modules.title: Development Economics
modules.objectives: Compare development indicators (HDI, GDP per capita, Gini coefficient); explain poverty traps; evaluate the effectiveness of foreign aid versus trade-led development.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Economics #21 — Economic Development & Poverty
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Economic Growth reading & questions
modules.assignment: Choose one low-income country and one upper-middle-income country. Compare their HDI scores, life expectancy, and literacy rates. Argue whether the lower-income country should prioritize aid or trade to accelerate development.

modules.title: International Monetary System
modules.objectives: Explain how exchange rates are determined; describe the roles of the IMF and World Bank; analyze the causes and consequences of currency crises.
modules.readingNote: CFR — IMF and World Bank Explained
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How do currency exchange rates work?
modules.assignment: Research the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis or the 1998 Russian ruble crisis. In 300 words, explain the causes of the currency crisis and evaluate the IMF's response.

modules.title: Geopolitics: Power & Influence
modules.titleZh: 地缘政治：权力与影响力
modules.objectives: Contrast realist and liberal theories of international relations; distinguish hard power from soft power; map major spheres of influence in the contemporary world.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course World History #39 — Realism vs Liberalism
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Soft power vs hard power explained
modules.practiceNote: BBC — Understanding Soft Power in the 21st Century
modules.assignment: Compare the soft power strategies of the United States and China. Give two specific examples for each country (e.g., cultural exports, foreign investment, international aid programs).

modules.title: International Organizations
modules.objectives: Describe the structure and purpose of the UN, NATO, WTO, EU, and ASEAN; evaluate their effectiveness in resolving international disputes and promoting cooperation.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course World History #39 — International Organizations
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How the United Nations works
modules.assignment: Evaluate the UN Security Council: Is its current structure (five permanent members with veto power) still appropriate for the 21st century? Write a 300-word argument for or against reform.

modules.title: Conflict & Security
modules.objectives: Analyze major causes of war (resource competition, nationalism, ideology); explain proxy conflicts during the Cold War and today; evaluate nuclear deterrence and UN peacekeeping.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course World History #39 — The Cold War and Proxy Wars
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How does nuclear deterrence work?
modules.practiceNote: BBC — Proxy Wars in the Middle East Explained
modules.assignment: Choose one ongoing armed conflict (e.g., Ukraine-Russia, Sudan, or another from CFR's tracker). Identify the main parties, the root causes, and the international response. Suggest one realistic policy recommendation.

modules.title: Human Rights & International Law
modules.objectives: Explain the origin and key provisions of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights; describe the role of the ICC; evaluate the concept of humanitarian intervention.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Government #39 — International Human Rights
modules.practiceNote: BBC — The Universal Declaration of Human Rights Explained
modules.assignment: Research one case before the International Criminal Court (e.g., a leader charged with crimes against humanity). In 300 words, explain the charges, the outcome or current status, and whether you believe the ICC was effective.

modules.title: Environmental Politics
modules.objectives: Explain the tragedy of the commons; analyze the Paris Agreement and its limitations; evaluate challenges of global environmental governance.
modules.readingNote: CFR — The Paris Agreement on Climate Change
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — The tragedy of the commons
modules.practiceNote: BBC — Paris Climate Agreement: What you need to know
modules.assignment: Is the Paris Agreement enough to prevent catastrophic climate change? Write a 300-word argument drawing on the concept of the tragedy of the commons and the specific commitments (or lack thereof) of major emitters.

modules.title: China's Rise & U.S.-China Relations
modules.objectives: Describe China's economic development model; explain the Belt and Road Initiative; analyze key flashpoints in U.S.-China relations including trade war and Taiwan.
modules.readingNote: CFR — China's Belt and Road Initiative
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Vox: How China became a superpower
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Why is China building islands in the South China Sea?
modules.practiceNote: BBC — China's Belt and Road Initiative Explained
modules.assignment: Should the United States treat China primarily as a strategic rival or an economic partner? Write a 350-word position essay using evidence from trade statistics, military posture, and diplomatic incidents.

modules.title: The Middle East: Oil, Politics & Conflict
modules.objectives: Explain how oil wealth shapes political systems in the Middle East; analyze the role of sectarianism; evaluate U.S. and outside involvement; examine the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.
modules.videoNote: Vox — The Middle East's cold war explained
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — A brief history of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict
modules.practiceNote: BBC — Middle East Conflict: Key Actors Explained
modules.assignment: Explain the concept of the 'resource curse.' Choose one Middle Eastern country (e.g., Iraq, Saudi Arabia, or Iran) and argue whether oil wealth has helped or hindered its political development.

modules.title: Africa & Emerging Economies
modules.objectives: Analyze Africa's demographic dividend and its economic potential; evaluate BRICS as a geopolitical grouping; explain the role of governance and debt in limiting or enabling development.
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How Africa can lead the next global economic boom
modules.practiceNote: BBC — Africa Rising: Facts and figures
modules.assignment: Some analysts predict Africa will be the world's next great economic growth story; others point to debt, governance failures, and climate vulnerability. In 300 words, argue which view is more convincing and why.

modules.title: The Future of Global Order
modules.objectives: Evaluate the shift from unipolarity to multipolarity; analyze how technology shapes geopolitics; assess threats to democracy and challenges for global governance reform.
modules.readingNote: CFR — The Future of World Order
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Kurzgesagt: The Rise of a New Superpower
modules.practiceNote: BBC — How Is the Global Order Changing?
modules.assignment: Write a 400-word essay: What will the international order look like in 2050? Will it be US-led, Chinese-led, multipolar, or something else? Support your prediction with evidence from current trends in economics, military power, and technology.

questions.question: Political economy is best defined as:
options: The study of how economic and political forces interact to shape policy and outcomes
questions.answer: The study of how economic and political forces interact to shape policy and outcomes

questions.question: In a command economy, key economic decisions are made by:
questions.answer: The central government

questions.question: Comparative advantage means a country should specialize in producing goods:
options: At which it has the lowest opportunity cost
That generate the highest absolute output
questions.answer: At which it has the lowest opportunity cost

questions.question: A tariff is best described as:
questions.answer: A tax on imported goods

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary benefit of globalization?
options: Access to larger markets and lower-cost goods
questions.answer: Access to larger markets and lower-cost goods

questions.question: The Human Development Index (HDI) measures:
options: Income, education, and life expectancy combined
questions.answer: Income, education, and life expectancy combined

questions.question: A poverty trap occurs when:
options: Low income prevents the investment needed to escape poverty
questions.answer: Low income prevents the investment needed to escape poverty

questions.question: When a country's currency depreciates, its exports become:
questions.answer: Cheaper for foreign buyers

questions.question: The IMF's primary role is to:
options: Provide short-term financial assistance to countries facing balance-of-payments crises
questions.answer: Provide short-term financial assistance to countries facing balance-of-payments crises

questions.question: Realism in international relations holds that:
options: States cooperate naturally for mutual benefit
International law is the primary guide to state behavior
States act primarily in their own national interest in an anarchic world
Global governance can replace state sovereignty
questions.answer: States act primarily in their own national interest in an anarchic world

questions.question: Soft power, as defined by Joseph Nye, refers to:
options: The ability to attract and co-opt rather than coerce
questions.answer: The ability to attract and co-opt rather than coerce

questions.question: The United Nations Security Council has how many permanent members (P5)?
NATO is primarily a:
questions.answer: Collective defense military alliance

questions.question: Explain the difference between absolute advantage and comparative advantage, and why comparative advantage is the stronger basis for international trade. Give a brief example.
questions.answer: Absolute advantage means producing more output with the same inputs; comparative advantage means producing at a lower opportunity cost. Comparative advantage is stronger because even a country that is worse at producing everything can still benefit from trade by specializing in what it gives up least to produce.

questions.question: What is the 'tragedy of the commons,' and how does it apply to global environmental challenges like climate change?
questions.answer: The tragedy of the commons is the tendency for shared resources to be overused because each actor benefits from use but shares the costs of depletion. In climate change, each country has an incentive to keep emitting while others reduce emissions, making collective action difficult without binding agreements.

questions.question: Which economic system relies most heavily on price signals and private property?
questions.answer: Market economy

questions.question: The World Trade Organization (WTO) primarily:
options: Provides development loans to poor countries
Creates rules for international trade and resolves trade disputes
questions.answer: Creates rules for international trade and resolves trade disputes

questions.question: Global supply chains allow multinational corporations to:
options: Source components and labor from wherever is most cost-effective
questions.answer: Source components and labor from wherever is most cost-effective

questions.question: Which indicator best captures inequality within a country?
questions.answer: Gini coefficient

questions.question: The World Bank's primary function is to:
options: Provide short-term currency loans during crises
Fund long-term development projects in developing countries
questions.answer: Fund long-term development projects in developing countries

questions.question: Liberalism in international relations emphasizes:
options: States competing for power in anarchy
Cooperation through institutions, democracy, and trade
Military dominance as the path to peace
questions.answer: Cooperation through institutions, democracy, and trade

questions.question: A proxy war is one in which:
options: Two superpowers fight each other directly
Major powers support opposing sides in a conflict fought by smaller parties
The UN intervenes to stop fighting
questions.answer: Major powers support opposing sides in a conflict fought by smaller parties

questions.question: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted by the UN in:
The Paris Agreement on climate change aims to limit global temperature rise to:
questions.answer: 1.5–2°C above pre-industrial levels

questions.question: China's Belt and Road Initiative primarily involves:
options: Military alliances across Asia and Africa
Infrastructure investment and loans to countries along trade corridors
A free-trade agreement modeled on the EU
questions.answer: Infrastructure investment and loans to countries along trade corridors

questions.question: The 'resource curse' refers to the paradox that:
options: Countries with many resources always develop faster
Natural resource wealth often correlates with weak governance and slow development
Oil-rich countries tend to have stronger democracies
questions.answer: Natural resource wealth often correlates with weak governance and slow development

questions.question: BRICS is a grouping of major emerging economies that includes Brazil, Russia, India, China, and:
questions.answer: South Africa

questions.question: Democratic backsliding refers to:
options: Countries transitioning from authoritarianism to democracy
The gradual erosion of democratic norms and institutions in previously democratic states
Electoral fraud in emerging economies only
The decline of voter turnout in established democracies
questions.answer: The gradual erosion of democratic norms and institutions in previously democratic states

questions.question: Nuclear deterrence theory holds that nuclear war is prevented by:
options: The UN's authority to disarm states
The mutual assurance that a nuclear attack will trigger devastating retaliation
questions.answer: The mutual assurance that a nuclear attack will trigger devastating retaliation

questions.question: Africa's 'demographic dividend' refers to the economic growth potential from:
options: Large reserves of oil and natural gas
A young, growing labor force that exceeds dependents
questions.answer: A young, growing labor force that exceeds dependents

questions.question: Multipolarity in the international system means:
options: One dominant superpower sets global rules
Two superpowers balance each other's power
Multiple major powers share influence without a single hegemon
International organizations govern all states equally
questions.answer: Multiple major powers share influence without a single hegemon

questions.question: The International Criminal Court (ICC) prosecutes individuals for:
options: Genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity
questions.answer: Genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity

questions.question: Explain what humanitarian intervention is and describe one argument for and one argument against it using a real historical example.
questions.answer: Humanitarian intervention is the use of force by outside states or international bodies to stop grave human rights violations within a sovereign state. For: The 1999 NATO intervention in Kosovo stopped ethnic cleansing and is cited as a success. Against: It violates state sovereignty and can be used selectively for geopolitical motives, as critics argued about the Iraq War.

questions.question: What is the 'demographic dividend' and how could Sub-Saharan Africa leverage it for economic development over the next three decades?
questions.answer: The demographic dividend is the economic growth potential that arises when a large share of the population is of working age relative to dependents. Sub-Saharan Africa could leverage it by investing in education and vocational training, improving healthcare, expanding manufacturing and services to absorb workers, and creating stable governance to attract foreign investment.

questions.question: How has technology (internet, AI, social media, cyber capabilities) changed the nature of geopolitical competition in the 21st century? Give two specific examples.
questions.answer: Technology has shifted competition beyond territory and military force. Example 1: Social media enables information warfare — Russia's Internet Research Agency used Facebook and Twitter to influence elections and sow division in the US and Europe. Example 2: Cyber attacks allow states to disrupt critical infrastructure without firing a shot, as seen in the 2021 Colonial Pipeline ransomware attack or attacks on Ukrainian power grids attributed to Russian state actors.

quizQuestions.question: Political economy studies the relationship between:
options: Politics and economics in shaping policy
quizQuestions.answer: Politics and economics in shaping policy

quizQuestions.question: In a pure market economy, prices are determined by:
quizQuestions.answer: Supply and demand

quizQuestions.question: A command economy is most closely associated with:
quizQuestions.answer: The Soviet Union

quizQuestions.question: A mixed economy combines elements of:
quizQuestions.answer: Market and command systems

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following best describes 'laissez-faire' economics?
options: Heavy government regulation of all industries
Minimal government interference in the economy
Central planning of production and prices
quizQuestions.answer: Minimal government interference in the economy

quizQuestions.question: Absolute advantage means a country can produce a good:
options: Using fewer resources than another country
With better quality than all others
quizQuestions.answer: Using fewer resources than another country

quizQuestions.question: Comparative advantage was first developed by economist:
quizQuestions.answer: David Ricardo

quizQuestions.question: A trade quota limits:
options: The quantity of goods that can be imported
The countries a firm can export to
quizQuestions.answer: The quantity of goods that can be imported

quizQuestions.question: The WTO was established in:
Which of the following is an example of a non-tariff trade barrier?
options: A safety regulation that foreign goods cannot meet
quizQuestions.answer: A safety regulation that foreign goods cannot meet

quizQuestions.question: Globalization refers to the increasing:
options: Interconnection of economies, cultures, and governance across borders
Dominance of one country's culture worldwide
quizQuestions.answer: Interconnection of economies, cultures, and governance across borders

quizQuestions.question: Multinational corporations (MNCs) are companies that:
options: Operate in multiple countries with significant foreign assets
quizQuestions.answer: Operate in multiple countries with significant foreign assets

quizQuestions.question: A major criticism of globalization is that it:
options: Can increase inequality within countries and harm vulnerable industries
quizQuestions.answer: Can increase inequality within countries and harm vulnerable industries

quizQuestions.question: The containerization of shipping contributed to globalization by:
options: Dramatically reducing the cost of transporting goods
quizQuestions.answer: Dramatically reducing the cost of transporting goods

quizQuestions.question: Offshoring refers to:
options: Moving production from overseas back to the home country
Moving business processes or manufacturing to another country to reduce costs
Selling goods on foreign stock exchanges
quizQuestions.answer: Moving business processes or manufacturing to another country to reduce costs

quizQuestions.question: GDP per capita is calculated as:
quizQuestions.answer: Total GDP divided by population

quizQuestions.question: The Human Development Index combines:
options: Military strength, trade volume, and inflation
Population size, area, and natural resources
quizQuestions.answer: Income, health (life expectancy), and education

quizQuestions.question: Foreign aid critics argue that aid can:
options: Create dependency and undermine local markets
quizQuestions.answer: Create dependency and undermine local markets

quizQuestions.question: Microfinance programs aim to help the poor by:
options: Offering small loans to entrepreneurs without access to traditional banking
quizQuestions.answer: Offering small loans to entrepreneurs without access to traditional banking

quizQuestions.question: A poverty trap is most likely to persist when:
options: A country has abundant natural resources
Low incomes prevent saving, investment, and skill development
A country has free trade agreements
quizQuestions.answer: Low incomes prevent saving, investment, and skill development

quizQuestions.question: A floating exchange rate is determined by:
options: Supply and demand in currency markets
quizQuestions.answer: Supply and demand in currency markets

quizQuestions.question: When the US dollar strengthens against the euro, American goods become:
options: More expensive for Europeans to buy
quizQuestions.answer: More expensive for Europeans to buy

quizQuestions.question: The Bretton Woods system (1944–1971) pegged currencies to:
quizQuestions.answer: Gold via the US dollar

quizQuestions.question: IMF 'conditionality' refers to:
options: Policy reforms a country must implement to receive IMF assistance
Limits on how long a country can borrow
Requirements that loans be repaid in gold
quizQuestions.answer: Policy reforms a country must implement to receive IMF assistance

quizQuestions.question: A currency crisis occurs when:
options: A country runs a trade surplus
Investors rapidly sell a currency, causing a sharp depreciation
The IMF raises its lending rate
A country joins a currency union
quizQuestions.answer: Investors rapidly sell a currency, causing a sharp depreciation

quizQuestions.question: Realism in international relations argues that the international system is characterized by:
options: Economic interdependence as the primary force
quizQuestions.answer: Anarchy and self-help among states

quizQuestions.question: Hard power refers to:
quizQuestions.answer: Military force and economic coercion

quizQuestions.question: A 'sphere of influence' is a region where:
options: An outside power exerts significant political and economic control
quizQuestions.answer: An outside power exerts significant political and economic control

quizQuestions.question: The Monroe Doctrine (1823) asserted that:
options: Europe should colonize Africa, not the Americas
The Western Hemisphere was a US sphere of influence, off-limits to further European colonization
All nations have the right to self-determination
Trade should be free between all nations
quizQuestions.answer: The Western Hemisphere was a US sphere of influence, off-limits to further European colonization

quizQuestions.question: Joseph Nye coined the concept of 'smart power,' which combines:
quizQuestions.answer: Hard power and soft power

quizQuestions.question: The United Nations was founded in:
Which UN body has primary responsibility for international peace and security?
quizQuestions.answer: Security Council

quizQuestions.question: The European Union began primarily as:
options: A customs union and economic community to prevent war in Europe
quizQuestions.answer: A customs union and economic community to prevent war in Europe

quizQuestions.question: ASEAN (Association of Southeast Asian Nations) was founded to:
options: Promote economic growth and political stability in Southeast Asia
Create a common currency for Asia
quizQuestions.answer: Promote economic growth and political stability in Southeast Asia

quizQuestions.question: A major limitation of international organizations like the UN is:
options: They have too many enforcement powers
They rely on member states' consent and often lack enforcement mechanisms
They focus only on economic issues
They are funded by private corporations
quizQuestions.answer: They rely on member states' consent and often lack enforcement mechanisms

quizQuestions.question: A proxy war occurs when:
options: Two countries declare war on each other directly
Major powers fund and support opposing sides in a third country's conflict
quizQuestions.answer: Major powers fund and support opposing sides in a third country's conflict

quizQuestions.question: Nuclear deterrence is based on the concept of:
quizQuestions.answer: Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD)

quizQuestions.question: The primary cause of the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine was:
options: NATO's eastward expansion and Russian security concerns, combined with Russian imperial ambitions
A trade dispute over natural gas
quizQuestions.answer: NATO's eastward expansion and Russian security concerns, combined with Russian imperial ambitions

quizQuestions.question: UN peacekeeping forces are authorized by:
quizQuestions.answer: The UN Security Council

quizQuestions.question: The Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT) aims to:
options: Allow all countries to develop nuclear weapons
Prevent the spread of nuclear weapons and promote disarmament
Ensure nuclear energy access for all nations
Create a nuclear-armed UN peacekeeping force
quizQuestions.answer: Prevent the spread of nuclear weapons and promote disarmament

quizQuestions.question: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in:
The International Criminal Court (ICC) is located in:
quizQuestions.answer: The Hague

quizQuestions.question: Humanitarian intervention is controversial because it can conflict with the principle of:
quizQuestions.answer: National sovereignty

quizQuestions.question: The Responsibility to Protect (R2P) doctrine holds that:
options: States have the right to invade neighboring countries
The international community may intervene when a state fails to protect its citizens from atrocities
Only the UN Secretary-General can authorize military action
Human rights are purely a domestic matter
quizQuestions.answer: The international community may intervene when a state fails to protect its citizens from atrocities

quizQuestions.question: Genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity fall under the jurisdiction of:
quizQuestions.answer: The International Criminal Court

quizQuestions.question: The tragedy of the commons describes:
options: The decline of common law legal systems
Overuse of shared resources because individuals prioritize personal gain over collective sustainability
Economic inequality between urban and rural areas
quizQuestions.answer: Overuse of shared resources because individuals prioritize personal gain over collective sustainability

quizQuestions.question: The Paris Agreement was adopted in:
Carbon pricing is a policy tool that:
options: Bans the use of fossil fuels immediately
Makes it more expensive to emit carbon, incentivizing cleaner alternatives
Caps the total number of cars on roads
quizQuestions.answer: Makes it more expensive to emit carbon, incentivizing cleaner alternatives

quizQuestions.question: Which country is the world's largest emitter of CO2 as of recent data?
quizQuestions.answer: China

quizQuestions.question: A key challenge in global climate governance is:
options: Too many countries agreeing too quickly
Free-rider problems where countries benefit from others' reductions without cutting their own emissions
The absence of any international climate body
Lack of scientific consensus on climate change
quizQuestions.answer: Free-rider problems where countries benefit from others' reductions without cutting their own emissions

quizQuestions.question: China's rapid economic growth from the 1980s onward was fueled primarily by:
options: Export-led manufacturing, foreign investment, and state-directed industrial policy
quizQuestions.answer: Export-led manufacturing, foreign investment, and state-directed industrial policy

quizQuestions.question: The Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) is China's program to:
options: Build a military alliance across Asia
Invest in infrastructure across Asia, Africa, and Europe to expand trade and influence
Create a free trade zone with ASEAN
Promote Chinese cultural values through media
quizQuestions.answer: Invest in infrastructure across Asia, Africa, and Europe to expand trade and influence

quizQuestions.question: The U.S.-China trade war that escalated in 2018 involved:
options: The US imposing tariffs on hundreds of billions of dollars of Chinese goods, with China retaliating
A currency agreement pegging the yuan to the dollar
quizQuestions.answer: The US imposing tariffs on hundreds of billions of dollars of Chinese goods, with China retaliating

quizQuestions.question: The Taiwan Strait is a geopolitical flashpoint because:
options: Taiwan and China have been in military alliance since 1949
China claims Taiwan as its territory; the US has a commitment to Taiwan's defense
Taiwan holds the world's largest oil reserves
The strait is the world's main shipping route for grain
quizQuestions.answer: China claims Taiwan as its territory; the US has a commitment to Taiwan's defense

quizQuestions.question: China's political system is best described as:
options: An authoritarian single-party state led by the Chinese Communist Party
quizQuestions.answer: An authoritarian single-party state led by the Chinese Communist Party

quizQuestions.question: The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) controls oil prices primarily through:
quizQuestions.answer: Coordinating member countries' production levels

quizQuestions.question: The Sunni-Shia divide in Islam is relevant to Middle Eastern politics because:
options: It determines which countries belong to OPEC
It shapes political alliances and rivalries, particularly between Saudi Arabia (Sunni) and Iran (Shia)
It defines the border between Iraq and Iran
It is the primary cause of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict
quizQuestions.answer: It shapes political alliances and rivalries, particularly between Saudi Arabia (Sunni) and Iran (Shia)

quizQuestions.question: The Israeli-Palestinian conflict centers primarily on disputes over:
options: Oil rights in the Mediterranean Sea
Territory, sovereignty, refugees, and the status of Jerusalem
Water rights in the Nile River
quizQuestions.answer: Territory, sovereignty, refugees, and the status of Jerusalem

quizQuestions.question: The 'resource curse' suggests that countries rich in oil or minerals often experience:
options: Higher foreign direct investment and faster growth
quizQuestions.answer: Corruption, authoritarianism, and economic volatility

quizQuestions.question: The Arab Spring (2010–2012) was a series of:
options: Military coups backed by the US
Popular uprisings across the Arab world demanding political reform
Economic trade agreements among Arab states
Peace negotiations between Israel and its neighbors
quizQuestions.answer: Popular uprisings across the Arab world demanding political reform

quizQuestions.question: BRICS originally stood for Brazil, Russia, India, China, and:
quizQuestions.answer: South Africa

quizQuestions.question: Africa's demographic dividend refers to:
options: The economic potential of a young, rapidly growing working-age population
High levels of foreign aid flowing into Africa
quizQuestions.answer: The economic potential of a young, rapidly growing working-age population

quizQuestions.question: Debt distress in African countries is often linked to:
options: Borrowing from China and other creditors at high rates for infrastructure with insufficient returns
Trade surpluses with the European Union
quizQuestions.answer: Borrowing from China and other creditors at high rates for infrastructure with insufficient returns

quizQuestions.question: Good governance is considered important for development because:
options: Stable institutions, rule of law, and low corruption attract investment and enable growth
It eliminates the need for foreign aid
It automatically increases a country's HDI ranking
quizQuestions.answer: Stable institutions, rule of law, and low corruption attract investment and enable growth

quizQuestions.question: The African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) aims to:
options: Establish a free trade zone across all African nations to boost intra-African commerce
Grant African countries preferred access to EU markets
quizQuestions.answer: Establish a free trade zone across all African nations to boost intra-African commerce

quizQuestions.question: A unipolar world order describes a system in which:
options: No single country has dominant power
One country holds overwhelming power globally
International organizations govern all states equally
quizQuestions.answer: One country holds overwhelming power globally

quizQuestions.question: Democratic backsliding is most commonly associated with:
options: Elected leaders gradually concentrating power, weakening courts, and suppressing the press
The rise of new political parties
quizQuestions.answer: Elected leaders gradually concentrating power, weakening courts, and suppressing the press

quizQuestions.question: Cyber warfare differs from traditional warfare because:
options: It always kills large numbers of people directly
Attribution is difficult and attacks can be launched without formal declarations of war
It is covered by the Geneva Conventions in detail
quizQuestions.answer: Attribution is difficult and attacks can be launched without formal declarations of war

quizQuestions.question: Artificial intelligence is reshaping geopolitics primarily because:
options: Countries leading in AI may gain decisive economic, military, and surveillance advantages
It reduces the need for international trade
quizQuestions.answer: Countries leading in AI may gain decisive economic, military, and surveillance advantages

quizQuestions.question: Global governance reform is urgently needed because:
options: Current institutions work perfectly for all countries
Institutions built after WWII do not adequately reflect the power and interests of emerging nations or address new challenges like pandemics and climate change
The United Nations has too much power
Most countries prefer to act alone without international coordination
quizQuestions.answer: Institutions built after WWII do not adequately reflect the power and interests of emerging nations or address new challenges like pandemics and climate change
